name: nclib

metadata.name: nclib
metadata.summary: Netcat as a library: convienent socket interfaces

documentation:
nclib is a python socket library that wants to be your friend.

nclib provides:

- Easy-to-use interfaces for connecting to and listening on TCP and UDP sockets
- The ability to handle any python stream-like object with a single interface
- A better socket class, the Netcat object

  - Convenient receive methods for common socket usage patterns
  - Highly customizable logging
  - Interactive mode, connecting the socket to your stdin/stdout
  - Intelligent detection of socket closes and connection drops
  - Long-running functions cleanly abortable with ctrl-c
  - Lots of aliases in case you forget the right method name

- Mechanisms to launch processes with their in/out streams connected to sockets

  - Launch a process with gdb attached

- TCP and UDP server classes for writing simple python daemons
- A script to easily daemonize command-line programs

Documentation is available at https://nclib.readthedocs.io/ and source code is
available at https://github.com/rhelmot/nclib

If you are familiar with pwntools, nclib provides much of the functionaly that
pwntools' socket wrappers do, but with the bonus feature of not being pwntools.

classes.name: nclib.server.TCPServer

methods.name: nclib.server.TCPServer.close
documentation: Tear down this server and release its resources

A simple TCP server model. Iterating over it will yield client sockets as
Netcat objects.

```
param bindto

The address to bind to, a tuple (host, port)

param kernel_backlog

The argument to listen()
```

Any additional keyword arguments will be passed to the constructor of the
Netcat object that is constructed for each client.

Here is a simple echo server example:

```pycon
>>> from nclib import TCPServer
>>> server = TCPServer(('0.0.0.0', 1337))
>>> for client in server:
...     client.send(client.recv()) # or submit to a thread pool for async handling...
```

classes.name: nclib.server.UDPServer

methods.name: nclib.server.UDPServer.respond
documentation:
Send a message back to a peer.

```
param packet

The data to send

param peer

The address to send to, as a tuple (host, port)

param flags

Any sending flags you want to use for some reason
```

methods.name: nclib.server.UDPServer.close
documentation: Tear down this server and release its resources

A simple UDP server model. Iterating over it will yield of tuples of
datagrams and peer addresses. To respond, use the respond method, which
takes the response and the peer address.

```
param bindto

The address to bind to, a tuple (host, port)

param dgram_size

The size of the datagram to receive. This is
important! If you send a message longer than the
receiver's receiving size, the rest of the message
will be silently lost! Default is 4096.
```

Here is a simple echo server example:

```pycon
>>> from nclib import UDPServer
>>> server = UDPServer(('0.0.0.0', 1337))
>>> for message, peer in server:
...     server.respond(message, peer) # or submit to a thread pool for async handling...
```

classes.name: nclib.process.Process

methods.name: nclib.process.Process.poll
documentation: Return the exit code of the proces, or None if it has not exited.

methods.name: nclib.process.Process.wait
documentation: Wait for the process to exit and return its exit code.

methods.name: nclib.process.Process.send_signal
documentation: Send the signal `sig` to the process.

methods.name: nclib.process.Process.kill
documentation: Terminate the process.

methods.name: nclib.process.Process.launch
documentation:
A static method for launching a process.
Same rules from the Process constructor apply.

```
param sock

The socket to use for stdin/stdout. If None, will be a new set of pipes.
```

A mechanism for launching a local process and interacting with it
programatically. This class is a subclass of the basic `Netcat` object so
you may use any method from that class to interact with the process you've
launched!

```
param program

The program to launch. Can be either a list of strings,
in which case those strings will become the program
argv, or a single string, in which case the shell will
be used to launch the program.

param stderr

How the program's stderr stream should behave. True
(default) will redirect stderr to the output socket,
unifying it with stdout. False will redirect it to
/dev/null. None will not touch it, causing it to appear
on your terminal.

param cwd

The working directory to execute the program in

param env

The environment to execute the program in, as a
dictionary
```

Any additional keyword arguments will be passed to the constructor of
Netcat.

WARNING: If you provide a string and not a list as the description for the
program to launch, then the pid we know about will be associated with the
shell that launches the program, not the program itself.

*Example:* Launch the `cat` process and send it a greeting. Print out its
response. Close the socket and the process exits with status 0.

```pycon
>>> from nclib import Process
>>> cat = Process('cat')
>>> cat.send('Hello world!')
>>> print(cat.recv())
b'Hello world!'
>>> cat.close()
>>> print(cat.poll())
0
```

classes.name: nclib.process.GDBProcess

methods.name: nclib.process.GDBProcess.__init__
documentation:
---
param cwd: The working directory to execute the program in
param env: The environment to execute the program in, as a dictionary
param gdbscript: The filename of a script for gdb to execute automatically on startup
param program: |-
  The program to launch. Can be either a list of strings, in which case
  those strings will become the program argv, or a single string, in which
  case the shell will be used to launch the program.
param protocol: The socket protocol to use. 'tcp' by default, can also be 'udp'
param stderr: |-
  How the program's stderr stream should behave. True (default) will
  redirect stderr to the output socket, unifying it with stdout. False will
  redirect it to /dev/null. None will not touch it, causing it to appear
  on your terminal.
---

Any additional keyword arguments will be passed to the constructor of Netcat.

Like nclib.Process, but also launches gdb (in a new $TERMINAL, by default gnome-terminal)
to debug the process.

functions.name: nclib.simplesock.wrap
documentation:
A helper function to automatically pick the correct wrapper class for a
sock-like object.

classes.name: nclib.simplesock.Simple

methods.name: nclib.simplesock.Simple.recv
documentation:
Receive at most `size` bytes, blocking until some data is available.
Returns an empty bytestring as an EOF condition.

methods.name: nclib.simplesock.Simple.send
documentation:
Send as much of the given data as possible, returning the number of
bytes sent.

methods.name: nclib.simplesock.Simple.close
documentation: Close the stream.

methods.name: nclib.simplesock.Simple.closed
documentation: Whether the stream has been closed.

methods.name: nclib.simplesock.Simple.fileno
documentation:
The file descriptor associated with the stream. Should raise
`NetcatError` if there is not a single file descriptor to return.

methods.name: nclib.simplesock.Simple.shutdown
documentation:
Indicate somehow that there will be no more reading/writing/both to
this stream. Takes the `socket.SHUT_*` constants.

methods.name: nclib.simplesock.Simple._prep_select
documentation:
Return three tuples of all the raw python file objects that should be
selected over in order to determine if this stream is
readable/writable/in an exceptional condition.

The base class for implementing a simple, unified interface for socket-like
objects. Instances of this type should act like a simple unbuffered
blocking socket.

:ivar can_send: Whether this stream supports send operations
:ivar can_recv: Whether this stream supports recv operations

classes.name: nclib.simplesock.SimpleSocket
documentation:
A wrapper for sockets.

```
param sock

A python socket.socket object.
```

classes.name: nclib.simplesock.SimpleFile
documentation:
A wrapper for files from the python `io` module, including `sys.stdin`,
subprocess pipes, etc. If the file has an encoding, it will be discarded.

```
param fp

An io.IOBase object
```

classes.name: nclib.simplesock.SimpleDuplex
documentation:
A wrapper which splits recv and send operations across two different
streams.

:param Simple child_recv:  The stream to use for recving
:param Simple child_send: The stream to use for sending

If either of these parameters are None, that operation will be disabled
and generate exceptions.

classes.name: nclib.simplesock.SimpleMerge
documentation:
A stream that merges the output of many readable streams into one.

```
param children

A list of streams from which to read

type children

list of Simple
```

classes.name: nclib.simplesock.SimpleNetcat
documentation:
A wrapper for a Netcat object! Why? Just in case you want to do
Netcat-level instrumentation [logging] at a finer granularity than the
top-level.

```
param sock

A Netcat object.
```

classes.name: nclib.simplesock.SimpleLogger
documentation:
A socket-like interface for dumping data to a python logging endpoint.

```
param logger

The dotted name for the endpoint for the logs to go to

param level

The string or numeric severity level for the logging
messages

param encoding

simplesock objects are fed bytestrings. Loggers consume
unicode strings. How should we translate?
```

functions.name: nclib.select.select
documentation:
A select function which works for any netcat or simplesock object.
This function is a drop-in replacement for python's `select.select`.

The main advantage is that sockets with multiple backing file descriptors
are handled cleanly.
hey!!! did you know that you can have nested loops in list/dict/generator comprehensions????

classes.name: nclib.logger.Logger

methods.name: nclib.logger.Logger.connected
documentation:
Called with a tuple of the peer to indicate "connection established",
either as a client or a server

methods.name: nclib.logger.Logger.sending
documentation: Called to indicate that some data has been sent over the wire

methods.name: nclib.logger.Logger.buffering
documentation:
Called to indicate that some data has been received and inserted into
the buffer

methods.name: nclib.logger.Logger.unbuffering
documentation:
Called to indicate that some data is being extracted from the buffer
and returned to the user

methods.name: nclib.logger.Logger.interrupted
documentation: Called to indicate that a socket operation was interrupted via ctrl-c

methods.name: nclib.logger.Logger.eofed
documentation:
Called to indicate that reading from the socket resulted in an EOF
condition

methods.name: nclib.logger.Logger.requesting_send
documentation: Called to indicate that the user has asked to send all of some data

methods.name: nclib.logger.Logger.requesting_recv
documentation:
Called to indicate that the user has asked for a receive of at most n
bytes

methods.name: nclib.logger.Logger.requesting_recv_until
documentation:
Called to indicate that the user has asked to receive until a given
string appears

methods.name: nclib.logger.Logger.requesting_recv_all
documentation:
Called to indicate that the user has asked to receive all data until
close

methods.name: nclib.logger.Logger.requesting_recv_exactly
documentation: Called to indicate that the user has asked to receive exactly n bytes

methods.name: nclib.logger.Logger.interact_starting
documentation: Called to indicate that an interactive session is beginning

methods.name: nclib.logger.Logger.interact_ending
documentation: Called to indicate that an interactive session is ending

The base class for loggers for use with Netcat objects. Each of these
methods will be called to indicate a given event.

classes.name: nclib.logger.ManyLogger
documentation:
A logger which dispatches all events to all its children, which are other
loggers. You shouldn't have to deal with this much; it's used automatically
by the Netcat.

```
param children

A list of loggers to which to dispatch events
```

classes.name: nclib.logger.TeeLogger
documentation:
A logger which feeds a copy of the input and output streams to a given stream

```
param log_send

A simplesock object to log all sends to, or None

param log_recv

A simplesock object to log all recvs to, or None

param log_yield

Whether recv logging should happen when data is
buffered or returned to the user
```

classes.name: nclib.logger.StandardLogger
documentation:
A logger which produces a human-readable log of what's happening

```
param log

A simplesock object to which the logs should
be sent

param log_yield

Whether to log receives when they are recieved
and written to the buffer or when they are
unbuffered and returned to the user

param show_headers

Whether to show metadata notices about user
actions and exceptional conditions

param hex_dump

Whether to encode logged data as a canonical
hexdump

param split_newlines

When in non-hex mode, whether logging should
treat newlines as record separators
```

functions.name: nclib.netcat.merge
documentation:
Return a Netcat object whose receives will be the merged stream of all the
given children sockets.

```
param children

A list of socks of any kind to receive from

param kwargs

Any additional keyword arguments will be passed on to
the Netcat constructor. Notably, you might want to
specify , since by default you will not
be able to send data to a merged socket.
```

functions.name: nclib.netcat._xwrap
documentation:
like simplesock.wrap but will also *unwrap* Netcat objects into their
constituent sockets. Be warned that this will discard buffers.

functions.name: nclib.netcat.ferry
documentation:
Establish a linkage between two socks, automatically copying any data
that becomes available between the two.

```
param left

A netcat sock

param right

Another netcat sock

param ferry_left

Whether to copy data leftward, i.e. from the
right sock to the left sock

param ferry_right

Whether to copy data rightward, i.e. from the
left sock to the right sock

param suppress_timeout

Whether to automatically set the socks'
timeout property to None and then reset it at
the end

param suppress_raise_eof

Whether to automatically set the socks'
raise_eof property to None and then reset it at
the end
```

classes.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.__init__
documentation: Initializer functions

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat._parse_target
documentation:
Takes the basic version of the user args and extract as much data as
possible from target. Returns a tuple that is its arguments but
sanitized.

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat._connect
documentation: Takes target/listen/udp/ipv6 and sets self.sock and self.peer

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.add_logger
documentation: Add the given logger to the list of current loggers

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.remove_logger
documentation: Remove the given logger from the list of current loggers

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.close
documentation: Close the socket.
Socket metadata functionality

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.closed
documentation: Whether the socket has been closed by the user (not the peer).
inconsistent between sockets and files. support both

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.shutdown
documentation:
Send a shutdown signal for one or both of reading and writing. Valid
arguments are `socket.SHUT_RDWR`, `socket.SHUT_RD`, and
`socket.SHUT_WR`.

Shutdown differs from closing in that it explicitly changes the state of
the socket resource to closed, whereas closing will only decrement the
number of peers on this end of the socket, since sockets can be a
resource shared by multiple peers on a single OS. When the number of
peers reaches zero, the socket is closed, but not deallocated, so you
still need to call close. (except that this is python and close is
automatically called on the deletion of the socket)

<http://stackoverflow.com/questions/409783/socket-shutdown-vs-socket-close>

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.shutdown_rd
documentation:
Send a shutdown signal for reading - you may no longer read from this
socket.

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.shutdown_wr
documentation:
Send a shutdown signal for writing - you may no longer write to this
socket.

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.fileno
documentation: Return the file descriptor associated with this socket

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.settimeout
documentation:
Set the default timeout in seconds to use for subsequent socket
operations. Set to None to wait forever, or 0 to be effectively
nonblocking.

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.gettimeout
documentation: Retrieve the timeout currently associated with the socket

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat._send
documentation: Core socket data functionality

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat._recv
documentation:
one-shot recv with timeout.
all timeouts are expressed via raising errors.NetcatTimeout
we wait until data is ready and then recv.
TODO: this is not thread safe...

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat._recv_predicate
documentation:
this is the core function which ties together all the nclib features
it will buffer data and call the predicate function on the buffer
until it returns a positive integer: the amount to unbuffer.

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat._fixup_timeout
documentation: Public socket data functions

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.recv
documentation:
Receive at most n bytes (default 4096) from the socket

Aliases: read, get

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.recv_until
documentation:
Recieve data from the socket until the given substring is observed.
Data in the same datagram as the substring, following the substring,
will not be returned and will be cached for future receives.

Aliases: read_until, readuntil, recvuntil

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.recv_all
documentation:
Return all data recieved until connection closes or the timeout
elapses.

Aliases: read_all, readall, recvall

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.recv_exactly
documentation:
Recieve exactly n bytes

Aliases: read_exactly, readexactly, recvexactly, recv_exact,
read_exact, readexact, recvexact

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.send
documentation:
Sends all the given data to the socket.

Aliases: write, put, sendall, send_all

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.interact
documentation:
Connects the socket to the terminal for user interaction.
Alternate input and output files may be specified.

This method cannot be used with a timeout.

Aliases: interactive, interaction

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.recv_line
documentation:
Recieve until the next newline , default "n". The newline string can
be changed by changing `nc.LINE_ENDING`. The newline will be returned
as part of the string.

Aliases: recvline, readline, read_line, readln, recvln

methods.name: nclib.netcat.Netcat.send_line
documentation:
Write the string to the wire, followed by a newline. The newline string
can be changed by specifying the `ending` param or changing
`nc.LINE_ENDING`.

Aliases: sendline, writeline, write_line, writeln, sendln

This is the main class you will use to interact with a peer over the
network! You may instanciate this class to either connect to a server,
listen for a one-off client, or wrap an existing sock/pipe/whatever.

One of the following must be passed in order to initialize a Netcat
object:

```
param connect

the address/port to connect to

param listen

the address/port to bind to for listening

param sock

a python socket, pipe, file, etc to wrap
```

For `connect` and `listen`, they accept basically any argument format
known to mankind. If you find an input format you think would be useful but
isn't accepted, let me know :P

Additionally, the following options modify the behavior of the object:

```
param sock_send

If this is specified, this Netcat object will act
as a multiplexer/demultiplexer, using the "normal"
channel for receiving and this channel for sending.
This should be specified as a python socket or pipe
object.

Using sock_send will cause issues if
you pass this object into a context which
expects to be able to use its
.fileno().

param udp

Set to True to use udp connections when using the
connect or listen parameters

param ipv6

Force using ipv6 when using the connect or listen
parameters

param retry

The number of times to retry establishing a connection
after a short (200ms) sleep if it fails.

param raise_timeout

Whether to raise a  exception when a
timeout is received. The default is to return any
buffered data and set self.timed_out = True

param raise_eof

Whether to raise a  exception when EOF
is encountered. The default is to return any buffered
data and set self.eof = True

param loggers

A list of  objects to consume socket events
for logging.
```

The following options can be used to configure default loggers:

```
param log_send

Pass a file-like object open for writing and all
data sent over the socket will be written to it.

param log_recv

Pass a file-like object open for writing and all
data recieved from the socket will be written to it.

param verbose

Set to True to cause a log of socket activity to be
written to stderr.

param echo_headers

Controls whether stderr logging should print headers
describing network operations and exceptional
conditions.

param echo_perline

Controls whether stderr logging should treat newlines
as record separators.

param echo_hex

Controls whether stderr logging should produce a
hexdump.

param echo_send_prefix

A prefix to print to stderr before each logged line of
sent data.

param echo_recv_prefix

A prefix to print to stderr before each logged line of
received data.

param log_yield

Control when logging messages are generated on
recv. By default, logging is done when data is
received from the socket, and may be buffered.
By setting this to True, logging is done when data
is yielded to the user, either directly from the
socket or from a buffer. This affects both stderr
and tee logging.
```

Any data that is extracted from the target address will override the
options specified here. For example, a url with the `http://` scheme
will go over tcp and port 80.

You may use this constructor as a context manager, i.e.
`with nclib.Netcat(...) as nc:`, and the socket will be automatically
closed when control exits the with-block.

*Example 1:* Send a greeting to a UDP server listening at 192.168.3.6:8888
and wait for a response. Log the conversation to stderr as hex.

```pycon
>>> nc = nclib.Netcat(('192.168.3.6', 8888),
...        udp=True, verbose=True, echo_hex=True)
======= Connected to ('localhost', 8888) =======
>>> nc.send(b'\x00\x0dHello, world!')
======= Sending 15 bytes =======
>> 000000  00 0D 48 65 6C 6C 6F 2C  20 77 6F 72 6C 64 21     |..Hello, world! |
>>> response = nc.recv()
======= Receiving at most 4096 bytes =======
<< 000000  00 57 68 65 6C 6C 6F 20  66 72 69 65 6E 64 2E 20  |.Whello friend. |
<< 000010  74 69 6D 65 20 69 73 20  73 68 6F 72 74 2E 20 70  |time is short. p|
<< 000020  6C 65 61 73 65 20 74 6F  20 6E 6F 74 20 77 6F 72  |lease to not wor|
<< 000030  72 79 2C 20 79 6F 75 20  77 69 6C 6C 20 66 69 6E  |ry, you will fin|
<< 000040  64 20 79 6F 75 72 20 77  61 79 2E 20 62 75 74 20  |d your way. but |
<< 000050  64 6F 20 68 75 72 72 79  2E                       |do hurry.       |
>>> nc.send(b'\x00\x08oh no D:')
======= Sending 10 bytes =======
>> 00000F                                                00  |               .|
>> 000010  08 6F 68 20 6E 6F 20 44  3A                       |.oh no D:       |
```

*Example 2:* Listen for a local TCP connection on port 1234, allow the user
to interact with the client. Log the entire interaction to log.txt.

```pycon
>>> logfile = open('log.txt', 'wb')
>>> nc = nclib.Netcat(listen=('localhost', 1234), log_send=logfile, log_recv=logfile)
>>> nc.interact()
```
